romania.summary: În urologia oncologică modernă, miza nu mai este doar îndepărtarea unei tumori, ci și cât din calitatea vieții pacientului poate fi păstrată după intervenție: funcțiile esențiale, autonomia și, atunci când… Articolul CLERU 2026: noile standarde ale chirurgiei urologice moderne, la Cluj-Napoca. Congres internațional de chirurgie robotică, cu intervenții live Da Vinci și laparoscopie 3D în cazuri oncologice complexe apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: adepth infusion și Universitatea de Vest din Timișoara organizează joi, 28 mai 2026, de la ora 18:00, în Sala A01 a UVT, conferința susținută de filosoful Nolen Gertz, una dintre… Articolul Filosoful Nolen Gertz vine la Universitatea de Vest din Timișoara pentru o conferință despre tehnologie, nihilism și sens în era conectivității permanente apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Un poliţist din Capitală şi-a spart deja televizorul încercând să ajungă la rapperul american, pe care susţine că l-a văzut înăuntru. „Îmi vedeam liniştit de treabă, televizorul mergea în fundal pe ceva post de muzică şi […] The post Wiz Khalifa, urmărit general în România. Curtea de Apel Bucureşti a dat astăzi verdictul: se pare că, de fapt, ANAF îi datorează lui Robert Negoiţă 200 de milioane de lei şi nu invers, cum credea toată lumea. „Statul român e dator vândut la Negoiţă, scrie aici, […] The post Curtea de Apel a decis că de fapt ANAF îi datorează 200 de milioane lui Negoiţă şi nu invers appeared first on Times New Roman .

Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
romania.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

medical.title: HPV self-collection boosts screening completion and cuts pelvic exams by one-third
medical.summary: A Kaiser Permanente study published by NEJM Catalyst Innovations in Care Delivery provides real-world data on a new approach to cervical cancer screening: giving patients the option to skip the traditional pelvic exam and collect their own vaginal samples to test for the human papillomavirus (HPV)—the cause of nearly all cervical cancers. The study is the first within a U.S. health care organization to demonstrate the potential of large-scale, population-based HPV self-collection—both via mail and in clinics—to improve access to preventive care and overcome barriers to traditional screenings.

medical.title: Scientists make surprising discovery and find a possible new ally in the opioid crisis
medical.summary: Scientists at Bowling Green State University recently made a series of discoveries that could have wide-ranging implications for the future of the national opioid crisis. Through a partnership with the Toledo Lucas County Health Department, a research team led by BGSU biological sciences professor Hans Wildschutte, Ph.D., analyzed used hypodermic needles from the county's needle exchange program, Northwest Ohio Safe Services. Researchers wanted to better understand what narcotics actually were in the needles and determine if any non-viral pathogens were present.

medical.title: Keratin 17 drives resistance in pancreatic cancer, study finds
medical.summary: A national team of cancer researchers led by Drs. Kenneth Shroyer and Natalia Marchenko at Stony Brook Medicine, and Dr. Luisa Escobar-Hoyos of Yale School of Medicine, investigated the role of Keratin 17 (K17) in the most common form of pancreatic cancer. They discovered that the protein is a key driver for chemoresistance to gemcitabine, an agent that is often used to treat a wide range of cancers, including advanced tumors of the pancreas, lungs and breast. Their findings highlight K17 as a potential target for the development of novel treatments to address the most aggressive forms of cancer.

medical.title: New technique discovers previously unknown population of immune cells in the Alzheimer's brain
medical.summary: A newly developed microscopy technique allows, for the first time, the visualization of more than 30 protein markers simultaneously in the human brain and uses bioinformatics to analyze their spatial relationships. In the process, the researchers have discovered a previously unknown population of immune cells in the brains of Alzheimer's patients that is found almost exclusively in the vicinity of a specific type of pathological protein deposit.

medical.title: A well-liked best friend can reduce an isolated child's exclusion but not their withdrawal, study finds
medical.summary: Elementary school can be an unforgiving place for children who are socially isolated. A close friend can be critical in helping these kids through tough times. However, a new Concordia paper published in the journal Child Development shows that the effects of friendship depend on the friend's level of social capital—meaning benefits like inclusion, trust, popularity and support—and the type of isolation a child is experiencing.

medical.title: One in four doctors believe human preservation and future revival could work, but not without challenges
medical.summary: A new survey of U.S. physicians focuses on human preservation procedures and the feasibility of future revival. Ariel Zeleznikow-Johnston of Monash University, Australia, and colleagues present their findings in the study, published in the open-access journal PLOS One.

medical.title: How schizophrenia risk may begin: Gene changes reshape signaling in developing neurons
medical.summary: Researchers at King's College London have identified the biological nature and timing of changes in human cortical neurons caused by altering activity of a schizophrenia-associated gene in developing human neurons. This discovery links a genetic risk factor to cellular changes in neurons; an essential step for understanding the neurobiology of this mental illness and developing future treatments.

medical.title: Spatial transcriptomics guides inflammatory bowel disease research
medical.summary: A novel spatial transcriptomics atlas developed by Northwestern Medicine scientists may improve the understanding of niche cellular interactions in the gastrointestinal tract that promote the development of inflammatory bowel diseases, as detailed in a recent study published in Nature Communications.

medical.title: Tumor microenvironment features may predict immunotherapy response in rare cancers
medical.summary: A new study in Cell Reports Medicine from researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center identified key features that may help predict which types of rare cancers are likely to respond to immunotherapy.

medical.title: Study suggests novel way to protect aging brains after surgery
medical.summary: Many people experience temporary cognitive impairment immediately after surgery. In people over age 60, however, about 1 in 10 continues to experience deficits in learning, memory, and executive function for more than three months after the surgery, a condition associated with increased morbidity and reduced quality of life.

medical.title: New clarity on a little‑understood stage of childhood development
medical.summary: Adrenarche—an early, puzzling transition between childhood and adolescence—has long been clouded by inconsistent terminology across pediatrics, endocrinology, and puberty research. A new call for precision aims to change that in a Viewpoint paper by Lauren Houghton, Ph.D., assistant professor of Epidemiology at Columbia University Mailman School of Public Health published in JAMA Pediatrics.

medical.title: Vitamin C may help prevent cancer, according to study of dietary patterns and water quality
medical.summary: A new study from the University of Waterloo uses mathematical modeling to examine how Vitamin C affects chemical reactions in the digestive system that are linked to cancer development. Over the last several decades, North American diets have seen a steady increase in exposure to nitrates and nitrites: compounds found in cured meats as well as fruits and vegetables grown using polluted soil and water. While nitrates and nitrites play important roles in neurological and heart health, in the stomach, they can undergo a chemical reaction known as "nitrosation" and form chemicals that many scientists suspect increase cancer risk.

medical.title: How medical education can revive the physician–scientist pipeline
medical.summary: The physician–scientist has long occupied a unique place in medicine—bridging the laboratory and the clinic, translating scientific discoveries into innovative patient care. But that role is becoming increasingly rare. The number of physicians involved in research has steadily declined over the past 50 years, raising concerns about the future of a workforce critical to improving human health.

medical.title: Measuring shame through a combination of self-report, language and body posture may be clinically helpful
medical.summary: In stigmatized illnesses such as human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), shame and other negative self-conscious emotions are associated with suboptimal engagement in health care via stress and avoidance coping. However, shame is challenging to assess via self-reporting. Research has shown that people express emotions in different ways, including self-reported emotion, what they say (using shame-related words), and facial and body movements. While people may be self-conscious about expressing or revealing shame, combining ways to measure shame could be helpful in improving accurate assessment.

medical.title: The neural basis of thought symbols identified for the first time
medical.summary: If you ask a child to draw an animal that doesn't exist, they'll often cobble together components from real ones—say, the body of a seal with an elephant's trunk, four octopus arms, and one lizard eye.

science.title: NASA TechLeap Prize: Robotically Manipulated Payload Challenge
science.summary: The Robotically Manipulated Payload Challenge — the fifth in the NASA TechLeap Prize series — is a competition to advance persistent infrastructure for in-space servicing, assembly, and manufacturing. NASA Flight Opportunities invites applicants to propose a payload that can be manipulated by a robotic arm in low Earth orbit. Up to three winners will each receive up […]

science.title: Paper calls for biologists to rethink how they analyze the impact of climate
science.summary: A new paper calls for ecologists and evolutionary biologists to consider how organisms experience climate rather than how weather stations record it when doing climate–biology research. The paper, "Matching climate to biological scales," is published in the April 2026 edition of Trends in Ecology & Evolution. Postdoctoral associate David Klinges, an incoming assistant professor at Rutgers University, was the lead author, and Yale Peabody Museum curators David Skelly and Martha Muñoz were among the co-authors.

science.title: Educational analysis of students' performance uses dynamic approach to include life's variables
science.summary: Academic success at university could depend on the changing interaction between students' habits over time rather than fixed traits such as intelligence or total study hours. This conclusion is discussed in the International Journal of Computational Systems Engineering in a paper that challenges the conventional methods of predicting and measuring educational success.

science.title: 129,000 years of crocodiles: What we know about Australasia's ancient apex predators
science.summary: The sight of a saltwater crocodile basking on a mudbank is one of the most iconic and intimidating images of northern Australia. Yet the crocodiles that inhabit the region today are just the survivors of a much richer and stranger lost world.

science.title: How you map numbers in your mind isn't universal, even among people who read in the same language
science.summary: Imagine taking out a 12-inch ruler and finding that the number 12 is on the left side and the number 1 is on the right side. For most native English speakers, this would be disorienting. We are used to seeing the numbers move from smallest to largest, from left to right. When this layout flips, people struggle because the numbers are now in the "wrong" place.

science.title: Air-conditioning cools homes but may weaken climate action
science.summary: New research from Singapore University of Technology and Design and the Singapore-ETH Centre finds that private cooling may protect people from heat while reducing the perceived urgency of broader urban climate solutions—a pattern the researchers call "behavioral insulation."

science.title: The quantum key to seeing through chaos
science.summary: Researchers from the Institut des NanoSciences de Paris, the Kastler Brossel Laboratory and the University of Glasgow have developed an innovative method that renders a scattering medium transparent solely for information carried by entangled photon pairs, while the same medium remains completely opaque to classical light.

science.title: Climate change spurs weight gain in owl monkeys
science.summary: Azara's owl monkeys, a small primate species found in South America, are heavier today than those that lived a quarter-century ago, and evidence suggests that rising temperatures might have driven the weight gain, according to a Yale-led study of a wild population.

science.title: Discovery of new fossils in Northwest Canada changes view of early animal evolution
science.summary: Researchers have uncovered a remarkable fossil site in a remote part of Canada's Northwest Territories, offering unprecedented insight into the earliest evolution of complex animal life on Earth. Findings from the site represent life from the Ediacaran biota—soft-bodied organisms that lived on the seafloor more than 500 million years ago—and push back the origins of animal movement and sexual reproduction by 5–10 million years.

science.title: Cows can recognize familiar human faces and match them to voices
science.summary: Cows show a visual preference for new human faces over a familiar one and can match a known handler's voice to their face, according to a study published in the open-access journal PLOS One by Océane Amichaud of INRAE in Nouzilly, France, and colleagues.

science.title: Thousands of UK beekeepers submit honey to benefit environmental science
science.summary: Beekeepers and their honeybees can be invaluable participants in environmental surveys, according to a study published in the open-access journal PLOS One by Jennifer Shelton of the UK Center for Ecology & Hydrology and colleagues.

science.title: How climate change is destroying Arctic cultural heritage sites
science.summary: Climate change is rapidly destroying cultural heritage sites across the Arctic, as exemplified in a 17th century "whalers' graveyard" which provides invaluable insights into early whalers' way of life, according to a study published in PLOS One by Lise Loktu of the Norwegian Institute for Cultural Heritage Research and Elin Therese Brødholt of Oslo University Hospital, Norway.

science.title: Psyche Spacecraft Completes Mars Flyby
science.summary: NASA’s Psyche spacecraft completed its close approach of Mars on May 15, coming within 2,864 miles (4,609 kilometers) of the planet’s surface. During the flyby, it took this image and others. This representative color image, captured by Psyche’s multispectral imager instrument, features the double-ring crater Huygens and the surrounding heavily cratered southern highlands. This flyby […]

science.title: Disability compounds employment woes for people with criminal records, and vice versa
science.summary: Justice-impacted individuals with disabilities (JIID) are nearly 11 percentage points less likely to be employed than people with disabilities who have not interacted with the criminal justice system, according to a study out of Cornell's Yang-Tan Institute on Employment and Disability.

science.title: Insects in the city: Flowers alone may not be enough to sustain them
science.summary: What renders a city garden attractive to insects such as solitary bees, bumblebees and hoverflies? And how well do they pollinate plants in urban areas? A study by the Federal Research Institute for Forest, Snow and Landscape shows that insects can pollinate plants in the entire city. However, they still require more insect-friendly green spaces. The findings are published in the Journal of Applied Ecology.

science.title: NASA-developed AI Could Help Track Harmful Algae
science.summary: NASA scientists have developed an artificial intelligence tool to take on a longstanding challenge in ocean waters. In a study recently published in AGU Earth and Space Science, researchers reported the tool was able to fuse data from multiple satellites and detect harmful algal blooms that occurred in western Florida and Southern California. Severe blooms […]

science.title: Flying sick: One in three pilots reported working despite illness
science.summary: Many pilots and cabin crew members go to work despite suffering from mental or physical health issues. This is shown in a new doctoral thesis by Filippa Folke at Karolinska Institutet.

science.title: Astronomers uncover why some solar eruptions die
science.summary: A team of scientists has recorded one of the most detailed views ever of a failed solar eruption, a powerful blast from the sun that never broke free. Their work is published in the journal Nature Astronomy.

science.title: Overturning a 200-year belief: New surface design enables two distinct wetting states on a single substrate
science.summary: NIMS discovered a phenomenon in which droplets on a single solid surface exhibit both a "sticky" and "repellent" state simultaneously. Namely, the wetting behavior branches into two states. This is a discovery that overturns interface chemistry scientists' belief held for over 200 years that, on a non-textured surface, the wetting state is uniquely determined by solid/liquid combinations. Furthermore, the research team also clarified a universal surface design principle that causes this phenomenon. This research result was published in Advanced Materials Interfaces on April 2, 2026.

science.title: Why millions of Europeans vote one way nationally, and the opposite in Brussels
science.summary: Millions of voters deliberately back one party in national elections and another in European elections to better match their views, according to new research from the University of Surrey. In a study published in Politics and Governance, researchers argue that this behavior is not random and that it is a strategic response when voters feel their usual party does not fully reflect their beliefs.

science.title: NASA Releases Technology Priorities to Energize Space Industry
science.summary: NASA released the 2026 Civil Space Shortfall Ranking list on Wednesday, which integrates more than 400 responses from stakeholders including industry organizations, government agencies, and academia. Shortfalls refer to technology areas requiring further development to meet future exploration, science, and other mission needs. The goal of this document is to rank the space community’s most pervasive shortfalls to […]

science.title: New insights into how the human hand evolved from our ape-like ancestors
science.summary: The human hand is an evolutionary marvel. While other primates rely on their hands for locomotion and basic grasping, ours can shape tools, manipulate objects, and perform detailed tasks requiring great dexterity and precision. The evolutionary basis of this unique ability has long been debated, but a new anatomical clue to its origin has just been revealed in a paper published in the journal Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences.

science.title: Company location, rather than growth, may lead to higher investment returns
science.summary: Investors looking for higher returns may want to focus on a company's location instead of its growth potential, according to a new study led by researchers at Penn State. They found that portfolios built to consider company headquarter location and the housing market together could produce returns three times higher than portfolios focused on growth stocks alone.

science.title: I Am Artemis: Tim Goddard
science.summary: Listen to this audio excerpt from Tim Goddard, NASA open water lead: At the end of their mission around the Moon, NASA’s Artemis II astronauts were recovered from their Orion spacecraft by a team of U.S. Navy divers and NASA personnel. This included Tim Goddard, NASA open water lead, who helped guide the complex open […]

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Fundația Calea Victoriei oferă locuri gratuite la Școlile de Vară 2026 pentru studenți și masteranzi
mix_items.summary: Studenții și masteranzii care își doresc să investească în dezvoltarea lor profesională și personală pot beneficia, în această vară, de locuri gratuite la Școlile de Vară 2026 organizate de Fundația… Articolul Fundația Calea Victoriei oferă locuri gratuite la Școlile de Vară 2026 pentru studenți și masteranzi apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: CLERU 2026: noile standarde ale chirurgiei urologice moderne, la Cluj-Napoca. Congres internațional de chirurgie robotică, cu intervenții live Da Vinci și laparoscopie 3D în cazuri oncologice complexe
mix_items.summary: În urologia oncologică modernă, miza nu mai este doar îndepărtarea unei tumori, ci și cât din calitatea vieții pacientului poate fi păstrată după intervenție: funcțiile esențiale, autonomia și, atunci când… Articolul CLERU 2026: noile standarde ale chirurgiei urologice moderne, la Cluj-Napoca. Congres internațional de chirurgie robotică, cu intervenții live Da Vinci și laparoscopie 3D în cazuri oncologice complexe apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Lumea în 1001 de hărți. Noaptea Muzeelor la Muzeul Hărților
mix_items.summary: Programul de vizitare: sâmbătă, 23 mai, între orele 12:00 și 23:00 Muzeului Hărților: strada Londra nr. 39, București. Preț bilet: intrare liberă De Noaptea Muzeelor, sâmbătă, 23 mai 2026, între… Articolul Lumea în 1001 de hărți. Noaptea Muzeelor la Muzeul Hărților apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor
mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Filosoful Nolen Gertz vine la Universitatea de Vest din Timișoara pentru o conferință despre tehnologie, nihilism și sens în era conectivității permanente
mix_items.summary: adepth infusion și Universitatea de Vest din Timișoara organizează joi, 28 mai 2026, de la ora 18:00, în Sala A01 a UVT, conferința susținută de filosoful Nolen Gertz, una dintre… Articolul Filosoful Nolen Gertz vine la Universitatea de Vest din Timișoara pentru o conferință despre tehnologie, nihilism și sens în era conectivității permanente apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Doar 1 din 2 copii ar anunța poliția în cazul unui abuz. World Vision România: Copiii care raportează trebuie sprijiniți și încurajați, nu umiliți public.
mix_items.summary: În România, mulți copii încă se tem să vorbească despre abuzurile prin care trec. Conform unui studiu al Fundației World... Articolul Doar 1 din 2 copii ar anunța poliția în cazul unui abuz. World Vision România: Copiii care raportează trebuie sprijiniți și încurajați, nu umiliți public. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor
mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: 😂 Wiz Khalifa, urmărit general în România. Un poliţist şi-a spart deja televizorul încercând să-l prindă
mix_items.summary: Căutarea lui Wiz Khalifa e în toi de când acesta a fost dat în urmărire generală pe teritoriul României! Un poliţist din Capitală şi-a spart deja televizorul încercând să ajungă la rapperul american, pe care susţine că l-a văzut înăuntru. „Îmi vedeam liniştit de treabă, televizorul mergea în fundal pe ceva post de muzică şi […] The post Wiz Khalifa, urmărit general în România. Un poliţist şi-a spart deja televizorul încercând să-l prindă appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 În pat cu Trump
mix_items.summary: The post În pat cu Trump appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România
mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... Articolul World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: 😂 Curtea de Apel a decis că de fapt ANAF îi datorează 200 de milioane lui Negoiţă şi nu invers
mix_items.summary: Veştile bune se ţin lanţ pentru toată lumea, dar mai ales pentru primarul Sectorului 3! Curtea de Apel Bucureşti a dat astăzi verdictul: se pare că, de fapt, ANAF îi datorează lui Robert Negoiţă 200 de milioane de lei şi nu invers, cum credea toată lumea. „Statul român e dator vândut la Negoiţă, scrie aici, […] The post Curtea de Apel a decis că de fapt ANAF îi datorează 200 de milioane lui Negoiţă şi nu invers appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi
mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: NASA TechLeap Prize: Robotically Manipulated Payload Challenge
mix_items.summary: The Robotically Manipulated Payload Challenge — the fifth in the NASA TechLeap Prize series — is a competition to advance persistent infrastructure for in-space servicing, assembly, and manufacturing. NASA Flight Opportunities invites applicants to propose a payload that can be manipulated by a robotic arm in low Earth orbit. Up to three winners will each receive up […]

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: HPV self-collection boosts screening completion and cuts pelvic exams by one-third
mix_items.summary: A Kaiser Permanente study published by NEJM Catalyst Innovations in Care Delivery provides real-world data on a new approach to cervical cancer screening: giving patients the option to skip the traditional pelvic exam and collect their own vaginal samples to test for the human papillomavirus (HPV)—the cause of nearly all cervical cancers. The study is the first within a U.S. health care organization to demonstrate the potential of large-scale, population-based HPV self-collection—both via mail and in clinics—to improve access to preventive care and overcome barriers to traditional screenings.

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Paper calls for biologists to rethink how they analyze the impact of climate
mix_items.summary: A new paper calls for ecologists and evolutionary biologists to consider how organisms experience climate rather than how weather stations record it when doing climate–biology research. The paper, "Matching climate to biological scales," is published in the April 2026 edition of Trends in Ecology & Evolution. Postdoctoral associate David Klinges, an incoming assistant professor at Rutgers University, was the lead author, and Yale Peabody Museum curators David Skelly and Martha Muñoz were among the co-authors.

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: Educational analysis of students' performance uses dynamic approach to include life's variables
mix_items.summary: Academic success at university could depend on the changing interaction between students' habits over time rather than fixed traits such as intelligence or total study hours. This conclusion is discussed in the International Journal of Computational Systems Engineering in a paper that challenges the conventional methods of predicting and measuring educational success.

mix_items.title: Scientists make surprising discovery and find a possible new ally in the opioid crisis
mix_items.summary: Scientists at Bowling Green State University recently made a series of discoveries that could have wide-ranging implications for the future of the national opioid crisis. Through a partnership with the Toledo Lucas County Health Department, a research team led by BGSU biological sciences professor Hans Wildschutte, Ph.D., analyzed used hypodermic needles from the county's needle exchange program, Northwest Ohio Safe Services. Researchers wanted to better understand what narcotics actually were in the needles and determine if any non-viral pathogens were present.

mix_items.title: 129,000 years of crocodiles: What we know about Australasia's ancient apex predators
mix_items.summary: The sight of a saltwater crocodile basking on a mudbank is one of the most iconic and intimidating images of northern Australia. Yet the crocodiles that inhabit the region today are just the survivors of a much richer and stranger lost world.

mix_items.title: How you map numbers in your mind isn't universal, even among people who read in the same language
mix_items.summary: Imagine taking out a 12-inch ruler and finding that the number 12 is on the left side and the number 1 is on the right side. For most native English speakers, this would be disorienting. We are used to seeing the numbers move from smallest to largest, from left to right. When this layout flips, people struggle because the numbers are now in the "wrong" place.

mix_items.title: Keratin 17 drives resistance in pancreatic cancer, study finds
mix_items.summary: A national team of cancer researchers led by Drs. Kenneth Shroyer and Natalia Marchenko at Stony Brook Medicine, and Dr. Luisa Escobar-Hoyos of Yale School of Medicine, investigated the role of Keratin 17 (K17) in the most common form of pancreatic cancer. They discovered that the protein is a key driver for chemoresistance to gemcitabine, an agent that is often used to treat a wide range of cancers, including advanced tumors of the pancreas, lungs and breast. Their findings highlight K17 as a potential target for the development of novel treatments to address the most aggressive forms of cancer.

mix_items.title: Air-conditioning cools homes but may weaken climate action
mix_items.summary: New research from Singapore University of Technology and Design and the Singapore-ETH Centre finds that private cooling may protect people from heat while reducing the perceived urgency of broader urban climate solutions—a pattern the researchers call "behavioral insulation."

mix_items.title: New technique discovers previously unknown population of immune cells in the Alzheimer's brain
mix_items.summary: A newly developed microscopy technique allows, for the first time, the visualization of more than 30 protein markers simultaneously in the human brain and uses bioinformatics to analyze their spatial relationships. In the process, the researchers have discovered a previously unknown population of immune cells in the brains of Alzheimer's patients that is found almost exclusively in the vicinity of a specific type of pathological protein deposit.

mix_items.title: The quantum key to seeing through chaos
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the Institut des NanoSciences de Paris, the Kastler Brossel Laboratory and the University of Glasgow have developed an innovative method that renders a scattering medium transparent solely for information carried by entangled photon pairs, while the same medium remains completely opaque to classical light.

mix_items.title: Climate change spurs weight gain in owl monkeys
mix_items.summary: Azara's owl monkeys, a small primate species found in South America, are heavier today than those that lived a quarter-century ago, and evidence suggests that rising temperatures might have driven the weight gain, according to a Yale-led study of a wild population.

mix_items.title: Discovery of new fossils in Northwest Canada changes view of early animal evolution
mix_items.summary: Researchers have uncovered a remarkable fossil site in a remote part of Canada's Northwest Territories, offering unprecedented insight into the earliest evolution of complex animal life on Earth. Findings from the site represent life from the Ediacaran biota—soft-bodied organisms that lived on the seafloor more than 500 million years ago—and push back the origins of animal movement and sexual reproduction by 5–10 million years.

mix_items.title: A well-liked best friend can reduce an isolated child's exclusion but not their withdrawal, study finds
mix_items.summary: Elementary school can be an unforgiving place for children who are socially isolated. A close friend can be critical in helping these kids through tough times. However, a new Concordia paper published in the journal Child Development shows that the effects of friendship depend on the friend's level of social capital—meaning benefits like inclusion, trust, popularity and support—and the type of isolation a child is experiencing.

mix_items.title: Cows can recognize familiar human faces and match them to voices
mix_items.summary: Cows show a visual preference for new human faces over a familiar one and can match a known handler's voice to their face, according to a study published in the open-access journal PLOS One by Océane Amichaud of INRAE in Nouzilly, France, and colleagues.

mix_items.title: One in four doctors believe human preservation and future revival could work, but not without challenges
mix_items.summary: A new survey of U.S. physicians focuses on human preservation procedures and the feasibility of future revival. Ariel Zeleznikow-Johnston of Monash University, Australia, and colleagues present their findings in the study, published in the open-access journal PLOS One.

mix_items.title: Thousands of UK beekeepers submit honey to benefit environmental science
mix_items.summary: Beekeepers and their honeybees can be invaluable participants in environmental surveys, according to a study published in the open-access journal PLOS One by Jennifer Shelton of the UK Center for Ecology & Hydrology and colleagues.

mix_items.title: How climate change is destroying Arctic cultural heritage sites
mix_items.summary: Climate change is rapidly destroying cultural heritage sites across the Arctic, as exemplified in a 17th century "whalers' graveyard" which provides invaluable insights into early whalers' way of life, according to a study published in PLOS One by Lise Loktu of the Norwegian Institute for Cultural Heritage Research and Elin Therese Brødholt of Oslo University Hospital, Norway.

mix_items.title: How schizophrenia risk may begin: Gene changes reshape signaling in developing neurons
mix_items.summary: Researchers at King's College London have identified the biological nature and timing of changes in human cortical neurons caused by altering activity of a schizophrenia-associated gene in developing human neurons. This discovery links a genetic risk factor to cellular changes in neurons; an essential step for understanding the neurobiology of this mental illness and developing future treatments.

mix_items.title: Psyche Spacecraft Completes Mars Flyby
mix_items.summary: NASA’s Psyche spacecraft completed its close approach of Mars on May 15, coming within 2,864 miles (4,609 kilometers) of the planet’s surface. During the flyby, it took this image and others. This representative color image, captured by Psyche’s multispectral imager instrument, features the double-ring crater Huygens and the surrounding heavily cratered southern highlands. This flyby […]

mix_items.title: Spatial transcriptomics guides inflammatory bowel disease research
mix_items.summary: A novel spatial transcriptomics atlas developed by Northwestern Medicine scientists may improve the understanding of niche cellular interactions in the gastrointestinal tract that promote the development of inflammatory bowel diseases, as detailed in a recent study published in Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: Disability compounds employment woes for people with criminal records, and vice versa
mix_items.summary: Justice-impacted individuals with disabilities (JIID) are nearly 11 percentage points less likely to be employed than people with disabilities who have not interacted with the criminal justice system, according to a study out of Cornell's Yang-Tan Institute on Employment and Disability.

mix_items.title: Tumor microenvironment features may predict immunotherapy response in rare cancers
mix_items.summary: A new study in Cell Reports Medicine from researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center identified key features that may help predict which types of rare cancers are likely to respond to immunotherapy.

mix_items.title: Insects in the city: Flowers alone may not be enough to sustain them
mix_items.summary: What renders a city garden attractive to insects such as solitary bees, bumblebees and hoverflies? And how well do they pollinate plants in urban areas? A study by the Federal Research Institute for Forest, Snow and Landscape shows that insects can pollinate plants in the entire city. However, they still require more insect-friendly green spaces. The findings are published in the Journal of Applied Ecology.

mix_items.title: Study suggests novel way to protect aging brains after surgery
mix_items.summary: Many people experience temporary cognitive impairment immediately after surgery. In people over age 60, however, about 1 in 10 continues to experience deficits in learning, memory, and executive function for more than three months after the surgery, a condition associated with increased morbidity and reduced quality of life.

mix_items.title: NASA-developed AI Could Help Track Harmful Algae
mix_items.summary: NASA scientists have developed an artificial intelligence tool to take on a longstanding challenge in ocean waters. In a study recently published in AGU Earth and Space Science, researchers reported the tool was able to fuse data from multiple satellites and detect harmful algal blooms that occurred in western Florida and Southern California. Severe blooms […]

mix_items.title: Flying sick: One in three pilots reported working despite illness
mix_items.summary: Many pilots and cabin crew members go to work despite suffering from mental or physical health issues. This is shown in a new doctoral thesis by Filippa Folke at Karolinska Institutet.

mix_items.title: Astronomers uncover why some solar eruptions die
mix_items.summary: A team of scientists has recorded one of the most detailed views ever of a failed solar eruption, a powerful blast from the sun that never broke free. Their work is published in the journal Nature Astronomy.

mix_items.title: New clarity on a little‑understood stage of childhood development
mix_items.summary: Adrenarche—an early, puzzling transition between childhood and adolescence—has long been clouded by inconsistent terminology across pediatrics, endocrinology, and puberty research. A new call for precision aims to change that in a Viewpoint paper by Lauren Houghton, Ph.D., assistant professor of Epidemiology at Columbia University Mailman School of Public Health published in JAMA Pediatrics.

mix_items.title: Vitamin C may help prevent cancer, according to study of dietary patterns and water quality
mix_items.summary: A new study from the University of Waterloo uses mathematical modeling to examine how Vitamin C affects chemical reactions in the digestive system that are linked to cancer development. Over the last several decades, North American diets have seen a steady increase in exposure to nitrates and nitrites: compounds found in cured meats as well as fruits and vegetables grown using polluted soil and water. While nitrates and nitrites play important roles in neurological and heart health, in the stomach, they can undergo a chemical reaction known as "nitrosation" and form chemicals that many scientists suspect increase cancer risk.

mix_items.title: Overturning a 200-year belief: New surface design enables two distinct wetting states on a single substrate
mix_items.summary: NIMS discovered a phenomenon in which droplets on a single solid surface exhibit both a "sticky" and "repellent" state simultaneously. Namely, the wetting behavior branches into two states. This is a discovery that overturns interface chemistry scientists' belief held for over 200 years that, on a non-textured surface, the wetting state is uniquely determined by solid/liquid combinations. Furthermore, the research team also clarified a universal surface design principle that causes this phenomenon. This research result was published in Advanced Materials Interfaces on April 2, 2026.

mix_items.title: Why millions of Europeans vote one way nationally, and the opposite in Brussels
mix_items.summary: Millions of voters deliberately back one party in national elections and another in European elections to better match their views, according to new research from the University of Surrey. In a study published in Politics and Governance, researchers argue that this behavior is not random and that it is a strategic response when voters feel their usual party does not fully reflect their beliefs.

mix_items.title: NASA Releases Technology Priorities to Energize Space Industry
mix_items.summary: NASA released the 2026 Civil Space Shortfall Ranking list on Wednesday, which integrates more than 400 responses from stakeholders including industry organizations, government agencies, and academia. Shortfalls refer to technology areas requiring further development to meet future exploration, science, and other mission needs. The goal of this document is to rank the space community’s most pervasive shortfalls to […]

mix_items.title: New insights into how the human hand evolved from our ape-like ancestors
mix_items.summary: The human hand is an evolutionary marvel. While other primates rely on their hands for locomotion and basic grasping, ours can shape tools, manipulate objects, and perform detailed tasks requiring great dexterity and precision. The evolutionary basis of this unique ability has long been debated, but a new anatomical clue to its origin has just been revealed in a paper published in the journal Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences.

mix_items.title: How medical education can revive the physician–scientist pipeline
mix_items.summary: The physician–scientist has long occupied a unique place in medicine—bridging the laboratory and the clinic, translating scientific discoveries into innovative patient care. But that role is becoming increasingly rare. The number of physicians involved in research has steadily declined over the past 50 years, raising concerns about the future of a workforce critical to improving human health.

mix_items.title: Company location, rather than growth, may lead to higher investment returns
mix_items.summary: Investors looking for higher returns may want to focus on a company's location instead of its growth potential, according to a new study led by researchers at Penn State. They found that portfolios built to consider company headquarter location and the housing market together could produce returns three times higher than portfolios focused on growth stocks alone.

mix_items.title: Measuring shame through a combination of self-report, language and body posture may be clinically helpful
mix_items.summary: In stigmatized illnesses such as human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), shame and other negative self-conscious emotions are associated with suboptimal engagement in health care via stress and avoidance coping. However, shame is challenging to assess via self-reporting. Research has shown that people express emotions in different ways, including self-reported emotion, what they say (using shame-related words), and facial and body movements. While people may be self-conscious about expressing or revealing shame, combining ways to measure shame could be helpful in improving accurate assessment.

mix_items.title: The neural basis of thought symbols identified for the first time
mix_items.summary: If you ask a child to draw an animal that doesn't exist, they'll often cobble together components from real ones—say, the body of a seal with an elephant's trunk, four octopus arms, and one lizard eye.

mix_items.title: I Am Artemis: Tim Goddard
mix_items.summary: Listen to this audio excerpt from Tim Goddard, NASA open water lead: At the end of their mission around the Moon, NASA’s Artemis II astronauts were recovered from their Orion spacecraft by a team of U.S. Navy divers and NASA personnel. This included Tim Goddard, NASA open water lead, who helped guide the complex open […]

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Cand zic "imediat", ma refer la varianta mea optimista.

satire_ro.title: Eveniment rar într-un pub din Capitală! Primul bețiv care a nimerit tabla de darts în ultimii 28 de ani
